explanation: The property states that the atomic proposition 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' (a controlTraffic message is sent from the Local Node of the OLSR to DM_i) must only occur after both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components have been queried. This means it should never be the case that a controlTraffic message is sent before querying both sets. To model this, we forbid sending the message ('controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') unless both 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' have occurred sometime earlier (or simultaneously). We must ensure: globally, if the controlTraffic message is sent, then it is always after the queries. This is equivalent to: it is always not possible to send the message before the queries have occurred. This can be captured using the global formula that forbids controlTraffic messages until the two queries have occurred. Thus, the property is: before 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' have both occurred, 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' must not occur. We can use: G[0,] !(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi & !(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set))

The property says that at all times, it is not allowed to send a controlTraffic message from the Local Node of the OLSR to DM_i unless both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set have been queried. In other words, every time 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' occurs, it must have been preceded by both 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set'. To express the requirement that a control-traffic message is sent only after querying both components, we use the LTL formula G(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> F(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)), but to more strictly enforce the 'only if both have been queried' semantics, we need to ensure that at any time, control traffic is not sent unless both queries have happened previously. The right template for this is index 8: G (bool_exp1 -> G[0,] !bool_exp3). We set bool_exp1 to 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' and bool_exp3 to '!(local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set)'. This ensures that whenever a controlTraffic message is sent, it is only possible if both queries have already occurred and not before. The scope is global because the statement applies for every DM_i, which is covered by the indexing of the formulas.

This LTL property guarantees that, for every DM_i, the system only sends a controlTraffic message to the DM_i after having queried both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. We use template 95 with N_DURATION0 set to 1 (since durations must be strictly positive), meaning: globally, every time a controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs, it must be the case that, starting from one step before, (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) holds until the message is sent. Setting bool_exp1 = TRUE ensures the implication holds globally. All other expressions are left blank as they are not needed for this property. This matches the desired causality constraint described in the natural language input.

The property requires that the controlTraffic message (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) is sent only after both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set have been queried by the Local Node (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set). The template chosen enforces that globally, whenever the controlTraffic message is sent, it must be the case that both queries to the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set have occurred before it. Since the property is universal (for every DM_i), it is expressed globally. We formalize the 'only after' as 'if the controlTraffic is sent, then it must be that both queries have already occurred.' This is best captured by G (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> G[0,] !( (!local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set) | (!local_node_queried_duplicate_set) ) ), which reduces to G(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi -> G[0,] (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set) ), but the strict causality is best captured by prohibiting sending the controlTraffic message except after both queries. Therefore, we use a LTL template that prohibits sending the controlTraffic message before both preconditions are satisfied.

This property expresses that for every DM_i, the sending of a control-traffic message from Local Node to DM_i (represented by 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi') must be preceded by a query of both the MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set components (i.e., 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set'). This requirement is safety in nature: the message must not be sent unless these sets are queried first. Therefore, 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' must never occur before both 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' have occurred. In LTL terms, this is equivalent to stating: It is always the case that if 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' holds, then prior to that, both 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' were true. This can be encoded by forbidding 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' to occur unless ('local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' & 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set') has already happened. The most direct encoding uses a safety property that globally forbids sending the control-traffic message unless the queries have been performed. In this fragment, we ensure that 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' only happens if 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' both have already occurred. Since the template language is limited to future temporal logic, we use the obligation that 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' must be preceded by both queries, effectively stating as an invariant that it is never the case that the control-traffic message is sent and at least one of the queries has not happened before.

The property states that the control-traffic message (controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi) must be sent only after the Local Node has queried both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set (local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set). Therefore, at all times, it must not be the case that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi occurs before both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set have occurred. The most suitable pattern template is a 'trigger-before', ensuring that controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi can only occur after these queries. Here, we use the LTL pattern to prevent sending the control traffic message before both queries, expressed as: globally, if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi happens, then previously both queries must have taken place.

This property captures the requirement that a controlTraffic message must only be sent from the Local Node to DM_i AFTER the Local Node has already queried both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. This is a safety property that forbids sending the controlTraffic message before those two queries have occurred. To encode this, we state that it is always NOT the case that a controlTraffic message is sent AND EITHER the MPR Selector Set or Duplicate Set have not yet been queried. This is represented using a global negation template: G[0,] !(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi & (!local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set | !local_node_queried_duplicate_set)). This ensures that at any time, if controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi is true, then both queries must have previously occurred.

The property requires that the controlTraffic message must be sent only after both querying the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. That means, globally, it is forbidden to send a controlTraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi before both local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set and local_node_queried_duplicate_set have occurred. This is best formalized by stating that it is always forbidden to send the controlTraffic message AND NOT having already queried both components. Thus, the property can be captured by: G[0,] !(controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi & (!local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set | !local_node_queried_duplicate_set)). This encodes that at no time should the control traffic message be sent unless both queries have happened, for every DM_i.

The property requires that a controlTraffic message can be sent from the Local Node to DM_i only after the Local Node has queried both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. The LTL property globally ensures that whenever controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi becomes true, it must have been preceded by queries to both MPR Selector Set and Duplicate Set by the Local Node. This is enforced by requiring that, globally, if a controlTraffic message is sent, it is never the case that it happens before both queries occur. Here, bool_exp1 is set to 'controltraffic_msg_sent_local_node_to_dmi' (the sending action), and bool_exp3 asserts the negation of having already queried both relevant sets ('local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set & local_node_queried_duplicate_set'). We use template 8, ensuring that globally, whenever the message is sent, it is always the case that the queries have happened before ('G (bool_exp1 -> G[0,] !bool_exp3)').

The property says a controlTraffic message from the Local Node to DM_i must be sent only after querying both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set. In LTL, this is typically expressed by stating that whenever a controlTraffic message is sent, it was necessarily preceded by queries to both relevant components. A common way is to forbid sending the controlTraffic message until both queries have occurred (in the past), or - more simply in LTL - that it cannot be sent unless both have already happened. Here, we use a globally-forbidden-unless formulation: always, if a controlTraffic message is sent, then 'local_node_queried_mpr_selector_set' and 'local_node_queried_duplicate_set' must be true now or in the past. Since the template structure allows only use of current atomic expressions (not history), the closest enforceable property is: 'Globally, if a controlTraffic message is sent, then at this moment the Local Node has (already) queried both the MPR Selector Set and the Duplicate Set.'

Thus, we use Template 8 (G (bool_exp1 -> G[0,] !bool_exp3)), setting bool_exp1 to sending controlTraffic message and bool_exp3 to 'not yet queried MPR Selector Set or not yet queried Duplicate Set'. This expresses that at all times that a controlTraffic message could be sent, it is not the case that either query is missing (i.e., both have been performed).
